caption: Figure 1: Examples of various segmentation tasks on heterogeneous document datasets.
llm_description: A comparative visualization of eight diverse text recognition datasets, each labeled with its name and type: PubLayNet (Layout Analysis), MTHv2 (Historical Documents), SCUT-CAB-Logical (Historical Documents), CTW1500 (Scene Text Detection), M⁶Doc (Layout Analysis), cTDaR-Modern (Table Detection and Recognition), cTDaR-Archive (Table Detection and Recognition), SCUT-HCCDoc (Handwritten Documents), and Total-Text (Scene Text Detection). Each panel displays sample images from its dataset with overlaid bounding boxes highlighting recognized text regions.

caption: Figure 2: Network structure of the proposed DocSAM. DocSAM unify various document image segmentation tasks into one single model through instance and semantic query decomposition and interaction. Skip connections and norm layers are omitted for simplicity.
llm_description: This diagram illustrates a vision-language model architecture for document understanding, featuring pre-processing of text and images, a vision encoder (with deformable attention), and two decoders—a multi-scale decoder with self- and cross-attention blocks—and a hybrid query decoder—to jointly predict mask, class, and bounding box outputs from the combined visual and textual representations.

caption: Figure 3: Loss curves and on-the-fly validation during training.
llm_description: Training loss curves (left) show rapid convergence across all DocSAM variants, while on-the-fly validation mAP (right) reveals that methods like Curriculum Learning and Query Selection achieve superior performance, indicating these enhancements effectively boost model accuracy during training.

caption: Figure 4: Qualitative results on public document layout analysis benchmarks produced by our DocSAM model.
llm_description: A grid showcasing ten distinct document layout datasets (BaDLAD, CDLA, D4LA, DocBank, DocLayNet, ICDAR2017-POD, IIIT-AR-13K, M6Doc, PubLayNet, RanLayNet), each displaying a unique sample page with varied text blocks, images, tables, and color-coded regions to illustrate diverse layout structures for research and benchmarking.

caption: Figure 5: Qualitative results on public ancient and handwritten document segmentation benchmarks produced by our DocSAM model.
llm_description: A grid of ten annotated historical document images, each labeled with its respective dataset name (e.g., CASIA-AHCDB-style1, CHDAC-2022, MTHv2), showcasing diverse layouts and OCR segmentation results with colored bounding boxes highlighting text regions.

caption: across a wide range of document processing tasks, highlighting its potential for practical applications in various domains.
Figure 6: Qualitative results on public table detection and structure recognition benchmarks produced by our DocSAM model.
llm_description: A grid of 16 labeled images showcasing diverse table datasets and benchmarks for vision-language models, including FinTabNet, PubTabNet, ICDAR2013, cTDaR-modern, NTable-cam, STDW, and others — each displaying tables with varying layouts, languages (English, Chinese), and structures to evaluate model robustness across domains like finance, medicine, and technical documentation.

caption: Figure 7: Qualitative results on public scene text detection benchmarks produced by our DocSAM model.
llm_description: A grid of 20 diverse real-world text images showcasing various OCR datasets, including storefront signs, billboards, street signs, product packaging, and handwritten text, each labeled with its corresponding dataset name.

caption: Figure 8: Failure cases produced by our DocSAM model. “GT” means ground-truth and “DT” means detection results.
llm_description: This image displays a comparative evaluation of six document layout analysis models (DocLayNet, M6Doc, CHDAC-2022, cTDaR-modern, cTDaR-archival) and four scene text recognition datasets (ICDAR2023-HierText, CTW-1500, ICDAR2019-ArT, CTW-Public, ICDAR2017-MLT), arranged in three rows. Each model or dataset is shown with its corresponding Ground Truth (GT) and Detected Text (DT) outputs. The GT row features complex layouts like multi-column documents and dense tables. The DT row shows the models' attempts to segment and recognize text within these layouts. The bottom row presents real-world images — including smartphone screens, signs, logos, and street scenes — alongside their detected text overlays, demonstrating the models' performance on natural, varied visual inputs.
